items.title: ‘No end in sight’ for No Surprises Act backlog - Modern Healthcare
items.reason: The article addresses operational implementation challenges and backlogs in the No Surprises Act's Independent Dispute Resolution process, which directly impacts revenue-cycle workflows and claim resolution timelines.
items.summary: Modern Healthcare reports that the Independent Dispute Resolution (IDR) process under the No Surprises Act faces a significant and growing backlog with no clear resolution timeline. The backlog delays the resolution of out-of-network billing disputes that should be settled through the federal IDR process, creating operational challenges for providers and plans processing surprise medical bills. This directly affects revenue-cycle teams managing claim disputes, payment timelines, and balance-billing resolution, as unresolved IDR cases accumulate and extend the time to final reimbursement determinations.

items.title: Health plan settlement marks OCRs 20th ransomware and 14th Risk Analysis Initiative - Nixon Peabody
items.reason: The item reports a health plan settlement with OCR (Office for Civil Rights) involving ransomware, which constitutes regulatory enforcement action against a payer entity.
items.summary: The Office for Civil Rights (OCR) reached a settlement with a health plan regarding a ransomware incident, marking OCR's 20th ransomware-related enforcement action and 14th action under its Risk Analysis Initiative. The settlement represents regulatory enforcement against a payer for cybersecurity failures that affected patient data. This matters to revenue-cycle teams because OCR enforcement actions against payers can lead to operational disruptions, compliance costs, and changes in payer security policies that may impact claims processing and provider-payer data exchange workflows.

items.title: People with this Medicare plan could soon go out-of-network at UHealth hospitals - Miami Herald
items.reason: This reports on a potential policy change affecting payer network status and out-of-network designations at specific healthcare facilities, which directly impacts revenue-cycle operations through claims processing, reimbursement rates, and patient liability determinations.
items.summary: Medicare plan members could soon be required to go out-of-network when using UHealth hospitals under a potential policy change reported by the Miami Herald. This development affects how claims are processed and reimbursed for UHealth facilities, with implications for patient cost-sharing and provider revenue cycles. The change would shift patients to out-of-network status at these hospitals, triggering different reimbursement methodologies and billing practices that revenue-cycle teams must implement. This type of payer network policy directly impacts claim adjudication, denial rates, and collection strategies for affected healthcare providers.

items.title: Judge tosses UnitedHealth’s defamation lawsuit against the Guardian - HealthExec
items.reason: This reports a judicial ruling dismissing a lawsuit involving UnitedHealth, a major payer, which falls within payer litigation and enforcement scope, though the defamation claim itself is not directly revenue-cycle operational.
items.summary: A judge dismissed UnitedHealth's defamation lawsuit against the Guardian, a news organization. The ruling represents a legal loss for UnitedHealth and may affect the payer's ability to pursue similar media-related claims. While the underlying dispute involves reputational matters rather than direct claims-handling or network practices, the outcome is relevant to healthcare payers' litigation posture and regulatory exposure. Revenue-cycle teams should monitor this as it reflects broader legal challenges facing major insurers and may influence payer communication strategies that indirectly affect provider relations.

items.title: House Committee Advances Bipartisan Bills on Hospital Price Transparency, Medicare Advantage and Drug Pricing Reforms - Families USA
items.reason: The item addresses legislative proposals affecting Medicare Advantage payers and hospital pricing policies, which impact payer enforcement and regulatory framework, though it focuses on proposed legislation rather than active litigation or enforcement actions.
items.summary: The House Committee advanced bipartisan legislation addressing hospital price transparency, Medicare Advantage reforms, and drug pricing. The bills would impose new regulatory requirements on Medicare Advantage plans and establish transparency mandates affecting how payers and providers interact on pricing and coverage. These legislative proposals would directly impact payer compliance obligations and create new enforcement frameworks, making them relevant to healthcare revenue-cycle teams managing payer relationships and claims processing, though specific effective dates and financial thresholds are not detailed in this brief announcement.
